name: Philodendron 'Dean McDowell'
origin: Horticultural cultivar; derived from tropical South American Philodendron parentage (P. gloriosum × P. pastazanum)
aboutText: Philodendron 'Dean McDowell' is a celebrated horticultural hybrid believed to be a cross between Philodendron gloriosum and Philodendron pastazanum — two of the most admired ground-crawling Philodendrons in the collector hobby. It inherits the best qualities of both parents: the velvety deep green leaf surface and bold, contrasting pale venation of gloriosum, combined with the larger, broader leaf potential of pastazanum, on a terrestrial rhizomatous plant that creeps along the substrate rather than climbing. New leaves emerge rolled and tightly furled before unfurling to reveal the rich dark surface with strikingly prominent primary venation. Feeder roots emerge from nodes along the running rhizome. Selected specifically for foliage quality and performance in cultivation, 'Dean McDowell' is a compelling choice for collectors who prefer the earthbound grandeur of the crawling species to their climbing counterparts.

quickFacts.matureSize: Medium to Large (50-90 cm leaf span)

morphology.venation: Striking pale prominent primary venation on velvety deep green blade
morphology.growthHabit: Terrestrial, compact rhizome creeping along substrate with feeder roots at nodes

propagation.notes: Single-node cuttings root readily in moist sphagnum or water. Maintain 70%+ humidity and bottom warmth for best results.

careGuide.substrate: Chunky, well-draining mix: 40% orchid bark, 30% perlite, 20% sphagnum moss, 10% worm castings. A wide, shallow pot suits the crawling growth habit.
careGuide.watering: Water when the top 3–4 cm is dry. These terrestrial species are slightly more forgiving than climbers but still hate waterlogged roots.
careGuide.humidity: 65–80%. Consistent high humidity produces larger, better-formed leaves.
careGuide.fertilising: Quarter-strength balanced fertiliser every two weeks during the growing season. Ease off in winter.
careGuide.repotting: Repot into a wider (not deeper) container when the rhizome reaches the pot edge. Every 18–24 months typically.

commonProblems.fix: Increase humidity and keep away from windows and vents in winter
Repot into fresh chunky substrate; trim any affected roots
Ensure 18–26°C and a brighter indirect light position

items: Confirm the rhizome is firm throughout — soft spots indicate rot
Check there is at least one healthy growing tip visible
Inspect leaf undersides for thrips (silvery stippling) and scale insects (small brown bumps on petioles)
Crawling philodendrons ship poorly if carelessly packaged — check seller reviews mention careful packing
